did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically six Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures (VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50), to classify dermatological disorders (Atopic, Eczema, and Psoriasis) from medical images. MobileNet achieved the highest accuracy of 95.7%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'Type: Original Research' in the metadata. It presents original experimental work involving the collection of a dataset (6723 images from Dermnet and University of Iowa), application of image filtering, and comparative evaluation of six CNN architectures (VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50) to classify dermatological disorders, reporting specific accuracy metrics (e.g., MobileNet 95.7%).

title: Deep Learning Based Classification of Dermatological Disorders
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of six Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures—VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50—for classifying three common dermatological disorders: atopic dermatitis, eczema, and psoriasis. The dataset comprised 6,723 images sourced from Dermnet and the University of Iowa, which underwent image filtering and noise reduction to enhance quality. Experimental results indicated that MobileNet achieved the highest classification accuracy of 95.7% with 100 epochs, outperforming the other architectures. The research highlights the potential of deep learning to support automated diagnosis for these specific skin conditions, particularly in regions like the Middle East where these disorders are prevalent. However, the authors note that limited local data and patient privacy concerns remain challenges for broader clinical adoption.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three diagnostic classes used in the classification experiment. Images labeled with this diagnosis were included from the University of Iowa repository.
One of the three diagnostic classes used in the classification experiment. Images labeled with this diagnosis were included from the University of Iowa repository.
One of the three diagnostic classes used in the classification experiment. Images labeled with this diagnosis were included from the University of Iowa repository.

all_task_types.target: Dermatological disorder categories (Atopic dermatitis, Eczema, Psoriasis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermatological disorders using six CNN architectures (VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50). The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for images sourced from Dermnet and the University of Iowa. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1 score, and MCC, with MobileNet achieving 95.7% testing accuracy.

datasets_used.name: Dermnet and University of Iowa Repository
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset sourced from Dermnet (founded by Thomas Habif, MD, in 1998, described as one of the largest independent photodermatology sources for medical education) and the Department of Dermatology at the University of Iowa (which offers a repository of disorders including cosmetic dermatology, skin remedies, and therapies). The images were collected to address the lack of local data for Middle Eastern dermatological disorders.

use.training: Used for training six CNN architectures (VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, ResNet50) with image filtering and noise reduction applied.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (val_step reported as 5 or 10 in experimental setups).
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (1934 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the models and compute the similarity/correlation between true and predicted values for symmetric classification.
To measure the efficiency and compare the performance of the six CNN architectures against each other.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1 score, and Matthew’s correlation coefficient (MCC) to compare the architectures.

experiment_3.name: epochs = 100, val_step = 5, steps = 10
experiment_3.notes: MobileNet achieved the highest performance with a loss value of 0.09.

experiment_6.name: epochs = 100, val_step = 10, steps = 10
experiment_6.notes: MobileNet achieved the highest MCC (95.4%) indicating perfect positive correlation. EfficientNet had the second highest MCC (91.2%).

experiment_7.name: epochs = 100, val_step = 5, steps = 10 (Per Class)
experiment_7.notes: EfficientNet produced higher values in Atopic. NasNet produced values for Eczema and Psoriasis close to MobileNet.

architecture_used.main_model: Six Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures: VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained models were used for image classification. VGG16 weights were initialized up to 16 layers. EfficientNet was built using the AutoML MNAS framework. MobileNet was based on depthwise and pointwise convolutions as described by Howard et al. NasNet was derived from neural architecture search. InceptionV3 and ResNet50 were also pretrained.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 x 3 (RGB images resized to 224x224 scale).

architecture_details.VGG16: Evaluated layer depth efficiency with weights up to 16 layers.
architecture_details.InceptionV3: Based on parallel connected layers instead of stacked layers; launched after InceptionV2 with improvements in factorization and fewer parameters (42-layer deep learning network).
architecture_details.ResNet50: Residual networks (ResNet) presented as a network-in-network architecture; block-based architecture with residual blocks where each layer connects input of a block with output to ensure nonlinearity.
architecture_details.MobileNet: Lightweight CNN architecture with 2 layers (filtering and combining); uses depthwise convolution (single filter on each input channel) and pointwise convolution (1x1 convolution to combine outputs).
architecture_details.NasNet: Neural Architecture Search network designed to automatically learn model architectures from the dataset; robust due to search space application on small datasets and transfer to larger datasets.
architecture_details.EfficientNet: Based on scaling up CNNs using compound coefficients where each dimension scales with fixed scaling coefficients; optimized baseline network built using neural architecture search via AutoML MNAS framework.

training_details.framework: Python Keras interface with TensorFlow backend.
training_details.preprocessing: BM3D filtering and denoising applied to enhance image quality and reduce noise before resizing to 224x224x3.
training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named, but MobileNet achieved a minimum loss value of 0.09 in the best-performing configuration (100 epochs, val_step=5).

classification_or_output_head.head_description: Predictors sent to predict the image class after convolution layers.

preprocessing_techniques: Image filtering and denoising using Block-Matching and 3D (BM3D) filtering algorithm to enhance image quality and reduce noise.
Resizing images to 224 x 224 x 3 (RGB scale).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper explicitly states that image augmentation was not used. The authors note: 'We have not used an image augmentation in this work.' They mention that while other works apply image augmentations to increase the number of images, this study relied on image filtering and denoising (BM3D) and resizing instead.

conclusions: MobileNet achieved the highest classification performance among the six tested CNN architectures (VGG16, EfficientNet, InceptionV3, MobileNet, NasNet, and ResNet50), reaching 95.7% testing accuracy with 100 epochs.
EfficientNet demonstrated the second-highest performance, particularly showing higher precision values for the 'atopic' class compared to other architectures.
The application of BM3D image filtering and denoising was utilized to enhance image quality and reduce noise, contributing to improved architecture performance.
The study highlights a limitation in the availability of local data for Middle Eastern dermatological disorders, which constrained the dataset scope.
The authors acknowledge that dermatological disorders share common visual features, making preprocessing crucial, and note that image augmentation was not used in this work.

Future work will focus on expanding the dataset to include new disorders and variations, as well as fostering collaborations between computer science practitioners and dermatologists to address data availability challenges.
important_remarks: The study is limited to three dermatological disorders (atopic dermatitis, eczema, and psoriasis) and does not address skin cancer, which dominates existing dermatological AI literature.

The dataset (6,723 images) was sourced from Dermnet and the University of Iowa, which may introduce population bias as it lacks local Middle Eastern data despite the study's focus on disorders prevalent in that region.
No image augmentation was used; the authors explicitly state reliance on BM3D filtering and denoising instead, which limits the model's robustness to variations in skin tone, type, and color.

The dataset is relatively small (1,934 testing images), and the authors acknowledge that limited local data and patient privacy concerns are significant barriers to broader clinical adoption and real-world deployment.
Dermatological disorders share common visual features, making preprocessing (BM3D filtering) crucial; however, the lack of diverse data and augmentation may hinder generalizability.
The study does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (p-values), relying instead on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1, MCC) for model comparison.
MobileNet achieved high training accuracy (99.3%) but lower testing accuracy (95.7%), indicating potential overfitting, although it still outperformed other architectures.

The authors note that most available dermatological images are for medical student illustrations rather than real clinical datasets, raising concerns about the clinical relevance and robustness of the findings.
